text.title: aws-s3-storage Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for aws-s3-storage. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Block all public access at both account level (S3 Block Public Access) and individual bucket level; set Object Ownership to BucketOwnerEnforced (disables ACLs); never grant public read via bucket policy.
rule.rationale: S3 misconfiguration (public access) is among the top causes of cloud data breaches. Account-level blocking provides a backstop even if per-bucket settings are misconfigured. ACLs are a legacy mechanism superseded by bucket policies.

rule.statement: Enable SSE-KMS with a customer-managed KMS key for buckets containing sensitive, regulated, or production data; use SSE-S3 (AES-256) for general non-sensitive buckets; enable S3 Bucket Keys to reduce KMS API call cost by up to 99%.
rule.rationale: SSE-KMS provides audit trails of key usage (CloudTrail), key rotation, and access policy control. SSE-S3 is free and requires no key management. Bucket Keys batch multiple object operations into a single KMS call.

rule.statement: Starting April 2026: SSE-C (customer-provided keys) is disabled by default for new buckets. If SSE-C is required, explicitly enable it via PutBucketEncryption API before any SSE-C upload.
rule.rationale: AWS changed the default to prevent misconfigured SSE-C usage where key management falls entirely on the customer with no AWS-side recovery. Teams migrating workloads must re-enable it explicitly.

rule.statement: Add a bucket policy denying all s3:* actions where aws:SecureTransport is false; this enforces HTTPS for all access including IAM principals with s3:GetObject permission.
rule.rationale: HTTP requests to S3 transmit data and credentials in cleartext. The SecureTransport condition denies HTTP even if IAM policies grant access, enforcing encryption in transit at the bucket level.

rule.statement: Use VPC Gateway endpoints for S3 access from EC2, ECS, Lambda, and EKS; configure endpoint policies to restrict access to specific bucket ARNs.
rule.rationale: Gateway endpoints are free and route S3 traffic over the AWS backbone rather than the public internet. Endpoint policies prevent the endpoint from being used to access unauthorized buckets.

rule.statement: Always add a lifecycle rule to abort incomplete multipart uploads after 7 days; add rules to expire non-current object versions and delete expired object delete markers.
rule.rationale: Incomplete multipart uploads accumulate indefinitely without a cleanup rule, silently adding storage cost. Non-current versions from versioned buckets accumulate without expiration rules — one bucket can grow to 10x the logical object size.

rule.statement: Transition objects to Standard-IA after 30 days (minimum), Glacier Instant Retrieval after 90 days, and Glacier Deep Archive after 365 days for logs and audit data; respect the 30-day minimum between transitions.
rule.rationale: Standard-IA costs 58% less than Standard but charges per GB retrieval and has a 30-day minimum storage charge. Transitioning too early (under 30 days) incurs early deletion fees that exceed the savings.

rule.statement: Enable S3 Intelligent-Tiering for buckets with unknown or unpredictable access patterns; enable the Archive and Deep Archive tiers for additional cost reduction on rarely accessed data.
rule.rationale: Intelligent-Tiering monitors access patterns and automatically moves objects between tiers with no retrieval charges. It eliminates the need to predict access patterns for lifecycle rules.

rule.statement: Enable versioning on all buckets containing production data or Terraform state; enable MFA Delete for buckets with compliance or financial data to prevent accidental version deletion.
rule.rationale: Versioning protects against accidental overwrites and deletes. MFA Delete adds a second factor to permanent version deletion — without it, a compromised access key can delete all versions permanently.

rule.statement: Enable CRR (Cross-Region Replication) for disaster recovery buckets with Replication Time Control (RTC) when RPO is under 15 minutes; source and destination must both have versioning enabled; configure an IAM replication role with least-privilege.
rule.rationale: Without RTC, replication time is best-effort (typically under 15 minutes but not guaranteed). RTC provides a 99.99% SLA for 15-minute replication, required for compliance-driven RPO targets.

rule.statement: Configure delete marker replication only when you need deletes to propagate to the replica; by default, delete markers are not replicated, preserving the replica as an independent backup.
rule.rationale: By default, CRR does not replicate delete markers — if a source object is deleted, the replica remains intact. This is correct for DR buckets. Enable delete marker replication only for active-active synchronization scenarios.

rule.statement: Verify on every new bucket: Block Public Access enabled (4 settings), Object Ownership = BucketOwnerEnforced, default encryption configured (SSE-KMS or SSE-S3), bucket policy with aws:SecureTransport = false deny rule.
rule.rationale: These four settings are the minimum security baseline for any S3 bucket. Missing any one creates a vulnerability: public access, ACL-based data leaks, unencrypted objects, or plaintext HTTP access.

rule.statement: For production buckets: enable versioning, enable S3 access logging to a separate audit bucket, enable CloudTrail data events, verify SSE-KMS with customer-managed key and S3 Bucket Keys enabled.
rule.rationale: Versioning enables point-in-time recovery. Access logging and CloudTrail data events are required for compliance audits (SOC2, PCI) to prove access to specific objects at specific times.

rule.statement: Configure at minimum: abort incomplete multipart uploads after 7 days, transition non-current versions to Standard-IA after 30 days, expire non-current versions after 90 days, delete expired object delete markers.
rule.rationale: These four lifecycle rules prevent unbounded bucket growth from three common accumulation patterns: aborted uploads, old versions, and orphaned delete markers.

rule.statement: For SSE-C: verify it is explicitly enabled via PutBucketEncryption after April 2026 for any buckets that require customer-provided keys; audit existing SSE-C buckets before the deadline.
rule.rationale: AWS disabled SSE-C by default for new buckets starting April 2026. Teams migrating or creating new buckets requiring SSE-C must explicitly opt in — silent failure otherwise.

rule.statement: For CRR: confirm versioning on source and destination, IAM replication role with s3:ReplicateObject and s3:ReplicateDelete, destination bucket policy allowing the source role, KMS key sharing if encrypted.
rule.rationale: CRR silently fails if any prerequisite is missing — there is no blocking error at configuration time. Replication metrics and CloudWatch alarms on replication failures are required to detect misconfiguration.
